text: The surgeon identifies the roux limb and observes an adhesive band a few centimeters past the gastrojejunostomy (GJ), causing visible compression on the roux limb.
The surgeon continues to run the small bowel, visually inspecting each segment to locate the exact site of obstruction.

The surgeon inspects the roux limb up to the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) and identifies a small defect, which is closed at the end of the procedure. However, this defect is not the cause of the small bowel obstruction.
The surgeon inspects the common channel of the small bowel, observing fecalization and potential partial obstruction by food mixed with contrast material.
The surgeon identifies the point of obstruction in the small bowel, where an adhesive band wraps around the proximal roux limb, causing extrinsic compression on the common channel.
The surgeon lyses an adhesive band causing the obstruction and continues to run the small bowel past the transition point, visibly relieving the obstruction.
The surgeon runs the distal small bowel towards the terminal ileum, visually inspecting the bowel for any signs of obstruction or abnormalities.
The surgeon observes the fecalization of the small bowel while tracing back through the intestinal tract, indicating potential obstruction or mixing of contents.
The attending surgeon performs the Moasis maneuver, milking the fecalized contents of the common channel towards the terminal ileum.

The surgeon gently opens the jaws of the instrument while pulling the small bowel, ensuring the intraluminal contents move distally without causing serosal injuries. The small bowel slides through the jaws, pushing the effluent towards the terminal ileum.
The surgeon pushes fecalized material into the terminal ileum, repeating the maneuver multiple times to ensure adequate decompression of the small bowel.
